title: The Waste Land — The Impure Path Without the Pure
subtitle: Eliot's poem as the portrait of the modern soul trapped in the kañcukas, unable to find the way through

summary.one_line: Eliot's The Waste Land as the anatomy of the modern soul trapped in the kañcukas without access to the pure path — a collection of tattva-fragments shored against ruin.
summary.scope: Covers the modern soul trapped in the kañcukas; 'These fragments I have shored against my ruins' as the collection of tattva-fragments; the Tarot as the tattva-system invoked but not understood; DA-DA-DA (Datta-Dayadhvam-Damyata) as the three yogas; 'Shantih shantih shantih' as the peace of the completed work.

body.text: The Waste Land is the portrait of the modern soul trapped in the kañcukas — the six limitations of consciousness — with no access to the pure path. Each section presents a different kañcuka in its contracted form: 'The Burial of the Dead' is the Earth tattva in its dead form (kalā — the corpse of limited agency); 'A Game of Chess' is the Water tattva polluted (rāga — attachment without love); 'The Fire Sermon' is Fire burning without light (vidyā — knowledge that does not know); 'Death by Water' is Air as dissolution (niyati — the end without purpose); 'What the Thunder Said' is the Ether as the barren sky (kāla — time that goes nowhere). The poem is a landscape of tattvas that have lost their connection to the śuddha path.

'These fragments I have shored against my ruins' (line 431) is the precise description of the soul on the impure path: it collects fragments of the tattvas — bits of the Upanishads, the Pervigilium Veneris, Dante, the Grail legend, Buddha's Fire Sermon — and uses them as a retaining wall against the collapse into complete dissolution. The fragments are the tattvas experienced as separate, disconnected pieces of a lost whole. The ruineruins are the anāśrita (the unsupported) — the soul's awareness that the ground beneath it is dissolving. The shoring is the desperate work of consciousness trying to hold itself together without the daimon that would integrate the fragments into a living whole.

Madame Sosostris' Tarot reading ('A Game of Chess' section) is the tattva-system invoked but not understood. The cards — the drowned Phoenician Sailor (death by water — dissolution of the Air/Ether principle), the Belladonna (the Lady of the Rocks — the Earth principle seductive and barren), the Wheel (time/kāla turning without purpose), the one-eyed merchant (partial knowledge/vidyā), and the Hanged Man (the sacrificed self/the sacrifice that is not understood) — are the tattvas appearing as fortune-telling fragments. The 'crowd of people, walking round in a ring' is saṃsāra: the endless circling of the tattvas without the liberating knowledge. The Tarot is the śāstra (doctrine) seen but not grasped.

The thunder's DA-DA-DA in 'What the Thunder Said' (the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad) gives the three yogas: Datta (give — the yoga of karma), Dayadhvam (be compassionate — the yoga of bhakti), Damyata (control — the yoga of jñāna). Each is heard by different beings (gods, men, demons) and each is the corrective to a specific kañcuka: Datta corrects kalā (limited agency — give what you have), Dayadhvam corrects rāga (attachment — release the ego), Damyata corrects asmitā (the 'I' — control the mind). But the modern soul hears them as echoes in a barren land — the teachings are present but the ground for their reception is dead. The 'dry grass singing' and 'the chapel' are the pure path glimpsed but not entered.

The hyacinth garden passage (lines 35-41) is the memory of the daimon — the moment of contact that was not sustained. 'You gave me hyacinths first a year ago' — the daimon's gift of the flower (the unfolding of consciousness). 'I could not / Speak, and my eyes failed, I was neither / Living nor dead' — the state of complete openness, the anāvṛtti (non-obscuration) that precedes the fall into the impure. 'Looking into the heart of light, the silence' — the glimpse of the śuddha path, the heart of the daimon where there is only silence. The rest of the poem is the exile from this moment — the long wandering in the waste land that follows the failure to sustain the daimonic connection.

The final 'Shantih shantih shantih' (the Upanishadic closing) is the peace that is not yet present in the poem but is invoked as the completion of the work. Eliot's note explains 'Shantih' as 'the peace which passeth understanding' — the śānti of the liberated state, the anuttara (the supreme) that is beyond all tattvas. The threefold repetition is the trika structure: emanation (creation), maintenance (preservation), and reabsorption (dissolution) — the cosmic cycle that closes the work of the poem just as it closes the work of the soul. The poem ends before the peace arrives — the Waste Land is the poem of the impure path, and 'Shantih' is the pure path invoked as a prayer, not experienced as a state.

gaps: Eliot's notes need separate treatment; the Grail legend subtext needs full analysis as the pure-path narrative embedded in the impure; line-level analysis needed

issues.title: Passages need refinement with specific line references
issues.description: All passages currently summarize themes without precise line-number citations.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. Six passages covering the soul trapped in kañcukas, fragments shored against ruins, Tarot as tattva-system, the thunder's DA as three yogas, the hyacinth garden as lost daimon, and 'Shantih' as the peace of the completed work.
